subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Dominoes uses a standard double-six set of twenty-eight tiles. Each tile is a small rectangular piece divided down the middle into two square halves. Each half displays a number of dots called pips, ranging from zero, which is blank, up to six. The set contains every possible pairing of those values exactly once, so you will find a zero-three, a two-five, a one-six, and so on. Seven of the twenty-eight tiles are called doubles. A double is a tile where both halves show the same number, like three-three or six-six. The doubles run from zero-zero all the way up to six-six. Doubles play a special role during the game, so it helps to recognize them at a glance. That is the entire set — no board, no dice, no cards. Just twenty-eight tiles and a flat surface.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: Dominoes does not use a fixed game board. Instead, you play on any flat surface large enough for the layout to grow as tiles are added. A table area of roughly three feet by three feet works well for most games. The layout begins with a single tile placed in the center and grows outward from there as players attach new tiles end to end. The chain of tiles forms a line that extends in two directions from the starting piece. When the special tile called the spinner is in play, the layout can also branch perpendicular to the main line, creating up to four arms radiating from the center. You may need to angle the line when it approaches the edge of the table, which is perfectly fine as long as the matching ends stay touching.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Begin by placing all twenty-eight tiles face down on the table and mixing them around thoroughly. This face-down pool of tiles is called the boneyard. Each player then draws tiles from the boneyard to form their starting hand. The number you draw depends on how many people are playing. With two players, each person draws seven tiles. With three players, each draws five. With four players, each draws five as well. Stand your tiles on their edges facing toward you so that you can see your own pip values but no one else can. The tiles remaining in the boneyard stay face down in the center of the table. They will come into play later when someone cannot make a match.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: The game begins with the player holding the highest double. Check first for the six-six. If no one has it, look for the five-five, then four-four, and so on down. The player who holds that highest double places it face up in the center of the table as the very first tile. This opening tile becomes the spinner, which is a special tile that can eventually be built upon from all four sides. If no player holds any double at all, everyone returns their tiles to the boneyard, reshuffles, and draws again. Once the first tile is down, play continues clockwise from the person who placed the spinner.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: Each turn follows a simple sequence. First, look at the open ends of the layout on the table and check whether any tile in your hand has a matching number. If you find a match, place that tile against the matching end so the identical numbers are touching. Your turn is now over. If nothing in your hand matches, you must draw from the boneyard. Take one tile at a time, adding each to your hand, until you draw one that matches an open end. When you find a playable tile, play it immediately and your turn ends. If the boneyard runs out before you find a match, you simply pass your turn without playing. Play always moves clockwise to the next person at the table.

subtopics.title: Matching and Drawing
subtopics.content:
The core action in dominoes is matching. To play a tile from your hand, one of its two halves must show the same pip value as an open end on the layout. You place your tile so that the matching halves are directly touching. For example, if an open end of the layout shows a four, you may play any tile that has a four on either half. Set it down with the four side against the open four, and the other half of your tile becomes the new open end on that arm of the layout.

The layout grows outward from the center as tiles are added end to end. At the start of a round only two open ends exist, one on each side of the spinner. As the spinner's branches open up, there can be as many as four open ends available for matching.

When you cannot match any open end, you draw from the boneyard. Pick up one tile at a time and check whether it plays. If it does, place it right away. If not, add it to your hand and draw again. You keep drawing until you find a playable tile or the boneyard is completely empty. In some common house rules, the last two tiles in the boneyard are left undrawn, meaning you must pass once the boneyard is down to two.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
The spinner is the first double played, and it is the only tile in the game that can be built upon from all four sides. However, the two in-line ends of the spinner must both be filled before anyone may attach tiles to the perpendicular sides. Once those first two extensions are in place, the spinner opens up two additional branches, giving the layout its characteristic cross shape.

All doubles, including the spinner, are placed crosswise, meaning perpendicular to the line of play. This is a visual convention that makes doubles easy to spot. Despite sitting sideways, a non-spinner double only has two open ends along the line, not four. Only the spinner grants the special four-way branching.

If the boneyard is empty and you hold no tile that matches an open end, you must pass your turn. Play moves to the next person clockwise. In a blocked game it is possible for several players in a row to pass, and the round ends when every player has passed consecutively because no one can play and no tiles remain to draw.

subtopics.title: Endgame and Scoring
subtopics.content:
A round ends in one of two ways. The first is when a player goes out by playing their very last tile. That player wins the round and scores points equal to the total pip count on every tile still held by all opponents. For example, if your three opponents are collectively holding tiles that add up to thirty-four pips, you score thirty-four points.

The second way a round ends is a blocked game. This happens when no player can make a legal play and the boneyard is empty. Everyone reveals their remaining tiles and counts their pips. The player with the lowest pip total wins the round and scores the difference between their total and the combined totals of all other players.

For a longer session, players continue round after round until someone reaches a target score, typically one hundred or two hundred points. That player wins the overall game. If you are playing casually or learning for the first time, a single round makes a perfectly satisfying complete game on its own.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: Your first priority in the early turns is to get rid of your heaviest tiles, meaning those with the highest pip totals. If the round ends in a block, every pip left in your hand counts against you, so shedding high-value tiles early reduces your risk. When you hold the six-six, leading with it as the spinner is ideal because it removes twelve pips from your hand in a single play. Follow up with your other heavy tiles like five-six or four-six whenever possible. Playing big tiles early also gives you a lighter, more flexible hand for the middle and late turns when precise matching becomes more important.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
One frequent error is hoarding several tiles that share the same number. If you hold three or four tiles featuring fives, for instance, you might assume you will always have a play when a five is open. But if the board blocks before you use them, you are stuck with a heavy hand. Spread your plays across different numbers instead of clinging to one suit.

Another mistake is failing to track which tiles have been played. There are only seven tiles containing any given number, so once you see five tiles with a three on the table, only two threes remain among all hands and the boneyard. Paying attention to this gives you a real information advantage.

New players also tend to ignore the boneyard count. Knowing how many tiles are left there helps you decide whether drawing is worthwhile or whether you should play a less ideal tile to avoid loading up your hand. Finally, watch your opponents when they draw. If someone draws three times when the open ends show a two and a five, they almost certainly lack both twos and fives. That knowledge can guide your plays for the rest of the round.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
The most important ongoing decision is controlling the open ends of the layout. When you have several tiles sharing a number, try to make the open ends display that number. This maximizes your future options while limiting what opponents can play. If you hold three tiles with sixes, playing so that both open ends show six gives you guaranteed plays on your next turn and forces opponents to either match a six or draw.

Another key decision is whether to race toward going out or to play for a block. If your remaining tiles have a low pip count, blocking the game might score you more points than going out quickly would. Compare the potential scores before committing to one approach.

Finally, know when a tactical draw is worthwhile. Drawing one tile from the boneyard to set up a strong play on your next turn can be more valuable than passing when the boneyard is empty. A single extra tile is a small cost if it lets you control the board or go out shortly after.

metadata.notes: Content rewritten to production quality. Standard Draw Dominoes with double-six set. Source: Domino Plaza and World Domino Federation. Redundancy between actions and special-mechanics resolved.
